content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

December 22, 1976

Mr. Donald M. Re
Attorney at Law
8383 Wilshire Boulevard
Suite 510
Beverly Hills, California 90211

Dear Mr. Re:

This is in response to your letter of December 2, 1976, concerning possible conflicts of interest on the part of the City Attorney and certain City Councilmen of the City of Lakewood.

Government Code §83114 provides:

Any person may request the Commission to issue an opinion with respect to his duties under this title ... (emphasis added)

The Commission and the staff consequently cannot issue opinions, nor provide advice pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code §18329, when the request concerns matters affecting individuals other than the person making the request or his client(s). See 2 Cal. Adm. Code §18320(a). We will not, therefore, comment in writing upon the situation you pose in your letter.

If, however, you wish to discuss generally the requirements of the Political Reform Act, I will be glad to assist you. Also, you may wish to consider contacting the appropriate District Attorney's Office and/or the Office of the Attorney General concerning this matter.

Thank you for writing.

Sincerely,

Kenneth W. Goshorn
Kenneth W. Goshorn
Research Specialist
Conflicts of Interest
Division

KWG:glb

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 • EXECUTIVE LEGAL 322-5901 • ENFORCEMENT CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6441


DONALD M. RE
ATTORNEYS AT LAW
8383 WILSHIRE BOULEVARD
SUITE 510
BEVERLY HILLS, CALIFORNIA 90211
(213) 655-5011

120 MONTGOMERY STREET
SUITE 2222
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94104
(415) 981-1801

December 2, 1976

Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California

Dear Sirs:

I wish to submit the following factual situation for your
consideration and opinion.

I am an attorney for Donald R. Plunkett, a City Councilman
for the Lakewood City Council. The Council is composed of
five members. The Council, by resolution, hires a private
attorney to act as a City Attorney. Since the time of the
City's incorporation, in 1962, there has been only one individual who has served as City Attorney. The City Attorney
advises the Counsel but does not cast a vote. The City
Attorney has held himself out as a "public official" in
various court proceedings.

Councilman Plunkett has filed a lawsuit against three other
council members (the majority of the Council) charging violation of the Ralph M. Brown Act [Government Code §§54950
et seq] and has alleged that the three City Councilmen met
at a non-public meeting and made a "collective decision" or
"collective commitment or promise" to make a positive vote
upon certain matters before the City Council. It is alleged
that this activity was taken in violation of the Brown Act.
Thereafter the Council voted on the question whether the
City Attorney should represent at the City's expense the
three City Councilmen named in the suit. The vote carried.
The three City Councilmen voted in favor of the City Attorney's
representation.

During the course of years, the City Attorney has prepared and
proposed a variety of resolutions regarding his fees as City
Attorney. The original agreement calls for a fixed monthly
salary. The most recent agreements have provided for a fixed
salary plus fees for "extraordinary fees and services". Each
resolution was prepared by the City Attorney and until several
weeks ago, no resolution defined in any way the term "extraordinary fees and services". When questions have arisen


I can make out that there are multiple paragraphs of text, but the specific words and content are too faint or blurry for me to transcribe with the accuracy your instructions require. Rather than guess at illegible text, I must indicate that the majority of this document's content is [illegible] due to the image quality.

If you have a higher contrast or clearer version of this document, I would be happy to provide a complete and accurate transcription.

Fair Political Practices Commission
December 2, 1976
Page 2


concerning the definition of "extraordinary" fees or services,
the City Attorney has advised the Council that various fees
he sought were properly within that definition.

The problems confronted by Councilman Plunkett are as follows:

1.     Was it a conflict of interest for the three City Councilmen
charged as Brown Act violators to vote to have the City Attorney
represent them in the suit?  The City is not a party to the action.
The suit is one against three City Councilmen personally.
Further, the suit is in reality brought for the benefit of the
City—i.e., should the allegations prove accurate, the City has
a definite interest in that finding.

2.     Is it a conflict of interest for the City Attorney to
(a)represent the alleged Brown Act violators and (b)to advise
the Council concerning the propriety of his representing the
three at City expense?  These fees are being charged as extra-
ordinary expenses and fees.

3.     Is it a conflict of interest for the City Attorney to ad-
vise the Council concerning the meaning of the terms "extra-
ordinary fees and services" when the contractual resolution,
prepared by the City Attorney, originally is ambiguous, and
with special view to the fact that he maintains a pecuniary
interest in the Council's determination?

Your attention to,and assistance in, these matters will be
greatly appreciated.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        DONALD M. RE


DMR:et
